macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Appointed Officials of the Temple

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and the shift in tone of the daf
First Mishnah: Elu hen hamemunim - The list of appointed officials
The officials overseeing receipts, libations, bird sacrifices, and digestive ailments
Petachiah identified as Mordechai and the challenge of dating these figures
Discussion of which generation these officials served and possible later glosses
Officials overseeing gates, wick-twisting, chimes, and choir
Beit Garmo and Beit Avtinas - houses that prepared showbread and incense

macro_segments.title: Gemara debate on recognition and merit of the officials

micro_segments.title: Rav Chizkiyah vs. Rabbanan: Are these the righteous of each generation or just who happened to be there?
Connection to Mishna in Yoma about zecher tzadik livracha vs. shem reshaim yirkav
Are Beit Garmo and Beit Avtinas righteous or wicked?

macro_segments.title: The organizing work of earlier generations in Torah and rabbinic traditions

micro_segments.title: Rav Yona cites Isaiah on dividing the spoil - Rabbi Akiva and general vs. particular
Anshei Knesset HaGedolah established halachot and aggadot through conceptual organization
Rabbi Abahu on Sofrim: The early rabbis numbered and structured the Torah like the Soferim
Mishnayot structured with numerical mnemonic devices as examples of organization
Recognition that Torah she-be-al-peh involves organizing and preserving traditions
Ezra the Kohen ha-Sofer: Just as early rabbis numbered their material, Ezra numbered the Torah

macro_segments.title: Parable of the bread-makers: The labor of previous generations

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Chagai quoting Rabbi Shmuel bar Nachman: The early ones plowed, planted, weeded, and prepared
We benefit from their work and yet we still struggle to access Torah

macro_segments.title: The comparison to angels and the story of Rabbi Pinchas ben Yair's donkey

micro_segments.title: If the earlier ones were angels, we are people; if they were people, we are donkeys
Rabbi Abba bar Zamina and Rabbi Ze'eira: We are not even like Rabbi Pinchas ben Yair's donkey
Story of Rabbi Pinchas ben Yair's donkey refusing food from thieves for three days
The donkey returns home and Rabbi Pinchas ben Yair feeds it with proper terumot and ma'asrot
The donkey's strict observance of halakha - it was machmir on itself

macro_segments.title: Petachiah: The interpreter of mysteries and hidden meanings

micro_segments.title: Petachiah's greatness: He could open closed things and knew seventy languages
Sanhedrin requirements: minimum, average, and wise Sanhedrin based on language speakers
Yavneh had four who could speak all seventy languages
Story of drought: A mute person pointed to indicate where to find omer, Petachiah interpreted
Second story: World-wide blight, another mute person, Petachiah interpreted correctly
Three women brought their korbanot with unclear intentions, Petachiah discerned the truth
Woman saying le-einati: danger in the eye, not zava status - a nedavah offering
Woman saying le-yamati: danger in the sea, not zava - a nedavah offering
Woman saying le-zivati: a wolf tried to take her child, not zava - a nedavah offering

macro_segments.title: Ben Achiah: Guardian of the Kohanim's health

micro_segments.title: Ben Achiah oversaw ailments of the stomach among the Kohanim
Kohanim went barefoot and ate vast quantities of meat, causing digestion problems
Ben Achiah knew which wines would help digestion and which would act as laxatives

macro_segments.title: Nechunya the well-digger and the death of his son in thirst

micro_segments.title: Nechunya dug wells and caves to provide water to the Temple and people
Nechunya knew which rocks would yield cold water
Nechunya knew the depth of hot water streams in the earth
Rabbi Eliezer: Yet Nechunya's son died of thirst - a theological problem
Rabbi Chanina: Whoever says God just lets things go - his stomach should also be let go
God extends wrath but ultimately collects what is owed - belief in divine accounting
Rabbi Acha: Those close to God are measured as precisely as a hairbreadth
Rabbi Yosi: God is awesome more upon those close than those far - greater awe, higher standard

macro_segments.title: The righteous chasid and his daughter washed away by a river

micro_segments.title: A chasid dug wells for travelers; his daughter was washed away on her wedding day
He refused consolation, maintaining his complaint against God's justice
Rabbi Pinchas ben Yair visits and asks: Is this your righteous man who won't accept God's justice?
Rabbi Pinchas ben Yair refuses to believe God would punish someone precisely in their virtue
The daughter miraculously appears alive - saved by grabbing a branch or an angel's intervention
Interpretation: Perhaps God would never allow such injustice, or Pinchas's intervention saved her
Contrast: One refuses to accept unjust suffering as fact; the other accepts it as divine will

macro_segments.title: Gevini the announcer and his thunderous voice

micro_segments.title: Gevini made announcements in the Temple to call everyone to their positions
His voice was so loud that King Agrippas heard it eight parsa away and gave him gifts

macro_segments.title: Ben Gever oversaw the locking of the Temple gates

micro_segments.title: Ben Gever locked the Temple gates at night, overseeing the Leviyim
Explanation of krias hagever: rooster crow or announcer's voice - linguistic debate

macro_segments.title: Ben Babi and the appointment of parnasin for charity distribution

micro_segments.title: Ben Babi oversaw the twisting of the wicks for the Temple menorah
Rabbi Yosi of Kfar urged appointment of parnasin for tzedakah but was refused
Rabbi Yosi encourages them: Those managing life-and-death matters of the poor deserve greatness

macro_segments.title: Ben Arza and the chimes signaling the Leviyim's song

micro_segments.title: Ben Arza struck the chimes to signal the Leviyim to begin their singing and music

macro_segments.title: Hugras ben Levi and the artistry of Temple song

micro_segments.title: Hugras ben Levi oversaw the singing of the Leviyim choir
Hugras knew an exceptionally beautiful melody and could produce many sounds with his thumb in his mouth
All the Kohanim were startled and amazed by the power and beauty of his music

topical_tags.term: Righteous of each generation vs. appointed officials
Zecher tzadik livracha vs. shem reshaim yirkav
Rabbi Akiva and organization of traditions
Labor metaphor - plowing and preparing bread
Rabbi Pinchas ben Yair's donkey and tzedaka consciousness
Son dies in thirst despite father digging wells
Hairbreadth measurement - midor as stringent on the righteous
Closeness to God and higher standards
Midah keneged midah - measure for measure
Angel appearing as Pinchas ben Yair
Krias hagever - rooster cry or announcer
Hugras ben Levi and the choir
